source: On The Tape / RiskReversal — Fear is the market killer
summary: Technology is a structural disinflationary force that is NOT changing, regardless of deglobalization
summary_extended: Baker argues that the 40-year disinflationary cycle was driven by two forces — technology and globalization — and that while globalization may be reversing due to deglobalization trends, technology's disinflationary role is 'fundamentally not changing.' He presents this as a structural, long-term view: regardless of geopolitical shifts or supply chain restructuring, technology will continue to suppress prices across the economy. The investment implication is that the secular bull case for technology as a category remains intact even in an inflationary macro environment, and that fears of a permanent inflation regime driven by deglobalization are overstated when technology is properly accounted for. Baker does not cite specific numbers or timeframes, but frames this as a multi-decade structural dynamic rather than a cyclical observation.
detail: Baker identifies two drivers of the 40-year disinflationary cycle: technology and globalization. He states unequivocally that technology's disinflationary role is 'fundamentally not changing,' even if globalization reverses. This is a structural long-term bullish view on tech's economic role.
confidence_evidence: Baker says 'Two things. One, technology and that's not changing. That is fundamentally not changing.' — stated with no hedging.
quote_fragment: technology and that's not changing. That is fundamentally not changing
